name: Vienna

seo.title: Vienna, Austria City Guide - Weather, Hotels, Flights & Events
seo.description: Plan a Vienna, Austria trip with city history, weather, hotel areas, airport access, sights, nearby places and events.

heroText: Vienna is Austria's capital on the Danube, known for imperial architecture, concert halls, museums, coffeehouse culture and efficient rail links across Central Europe.

highlights.name: Schonbrunn Palace
highlights.text: Former Habsburg summer palace with grand state rooms, formal gardens and a long park axis west of the centre.

highlights.name: St. Stephen's Cathedral
highlights.text: Vienna's Gothic landmark in the Innere Stadt, useful as a central meeting point, skyline marker, tower climb and first-orientation stop for the old town.

highlights.name: Hofburg
highlights.text: The imperial palace complex anchors central Vienna with museums, courtyards, the Spanish Riding School, state buildings and easy walks toward the Ringstrasse.

highlights.name: Belvedere
highlights.text: Baroque palace and museum complex near the main station, known for Austrian art collections, formal gardens and a route that pairs well with Wieden.

highlights.name: Prater
highlights.text: Large public park with the Riesenrad ferris wheel, amusement rides and broad paths for an easier outdoor break.

highlights.name: MuseumsQuartier
highlights.text: Dense museum and culture district near the Ringstrasse, practical when weather, contemporary art, cafes or short transfer times matter during a city stay.

history.text: The Roman military settlement of Vindobona stood on the site of today's central Vienna.
The Babenberg rulers made Vienna their residence, helping the settlement grow into a regional political centre.
Habsburg rule began to shape Vienna's long role as an imperial residence and administrative capital.
The failed Ottoman siege of Vienna became a decisive moment in the city's early modern history.
Vienna became capital of the Austrian Empire, reinforcing its role as a centre of court culture, administration and music.
After the Austro-Hungarian Compromise, Vienna remained capital of the Austro-Hungarian Empire.
After World War I, Vienna became capital of the new Austrian republic.
The Red Vienna period expanded municipal housing and social infrastructure, leaving major housing estates across the city.
Nazi Germany annexed Austria; Vienna's Jewish community was persecuted and much of its cultural life was destroyed.
Vienna was heavily damaged near the end of World War II and then divided into Allied occupation sectors.
The Austrian State Treaty restored full sovereignty, and Vienna continued as Austria's federal capital.
The Vienna International Centre opened, strengthening the city's role as a base for international organizations.
